Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered most of the time. I'd try to set aside time each day, but with limited resources at home and needing to do things hands-on to actually understand them, I found myself studying in bursts whenever I could. I'd spend hours some days just trying to work through problems physically, writing things out over and over. The 33 hours a week sounds like a lot, but it felt chaotic rather than organized.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what was due soon. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be cramming when deadlines approached. Regular schedules are hard for me because I don't have the best setup at home, and sometimes I just couldn't focus unless there was pressure pushing me forward. The fear of failing kept me going more than any real schedule did.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due or just this anxious feeling that I was falling behind. I logged in pretty regularly, like five times a week or so, mostly because I was scared of missing something important. The gamified tasks they added actually helped motivate me to check in more often because it felt less overwhelming when there were points and progress bars involved.
Q4: Yeah, after a while logging into the LMS became almost automatic, especially in the mornings. The gamified elements really helped with that because I'd want to see my progress and earn those little achievements. It gave me something concrete to work toward when my actual motivation was pretty low. It became part of my routine even though I wasn't always doing deep learning when I was there.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could pause them and actually try things out as I watched. I spent a lot of time on those, probably over four hours total. The discussion forums were also helpful because I could see how other students were working through problems, which helped since I learn better by doing and seeing examples. The gamified tasks made even boring content feel more approachable.
Q6: I often skipped the longer reading materials because I just couldn't sit still and absorb text the same way. PDFs and dense articles would sit there untouched for weeks. I'm a hands-on learner, so anything that was just walls of text felt like it wasn't going to stick anyway. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but rarely did unless they were absolutely required.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually do something with it, not just read or listen passively. If there was a practical component or if it connected to something I'd need for an assignment, I'd engage with it. The gamified stuff helped too because even small tasks felt rewarding when I could see progress. Without that feedback, everything just felt like an endless slog.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to spend time on, honestly. With my motivation being so low and stress being high, I had to triage everything. Required stuff got done because I was scared of the consequences, but optional things fell away unless they were genuinely interesting or had some game element attached. The pressure of required work made optional stuff feel almost guilty to spend time on.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download videos and watch them on my phone when I didn't have good internet. I'd write out notes by hand and practice problems on paper because that's how I actually learn things. A lot of my real studying happened away from the computer entirely, just me working through concepts physically.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be at my desk with printed notes, working through problems for hours, and none of that would register anywhere online. I'd download a lecture once and then replay it offline multiple times. My LMS activity probably looked minimal during those stretches, but I was actually grinding through the material in my own way. The system just couldn't see that kind of work.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty common for me. I'd access something once, download it if I could, and then study it offline for days. With my internet being unreliable sometimes and my resources being limited, it made more sense to grab what I needed and work with it locally. One video might show as a single view but I'd have watched it three or four times on my phone.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt impossible. When stress was really high, I'd just freeze up and couldn't make myself do anything productive. My attendance was really low because sometimes I just couldn't bring myself to show up, even online. The fear of failing was always there, but sometimes that fear paralyzed me instead of motivating me.
Q13: Definitely. When I had other responsibilities piling up or when I was just exhausted, the LMS became something I'd check quickly but not really engage with deeply. I'd log in, look at what was due, feel overwhelmed, and sometimes just log out without doing much. Fatigue especially hit hard because I need to be actively doing things to learn, and when I'm tired, that's impossible.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials or watching videos but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd finish a lecture and realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too stressed or distracted. Those sessions probably looked productive in the data, but they were basically wasted time. I was going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The fear would build up and instead of pushing me to work, it would make me avoid thinking about it altogether. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow I'd say the same thing. By the time I actually started, I'd be rushing and not doing my best work. It's why my assignment completion wasn't great.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me actually submit work. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly all that avoidance would turn into panic-fueled productivity. My LMS usage would spike right before things were due. It wasn't healthy, but without that external pressure, I'd just keep putting things off indefinitely. The fear of missing deadlines was more motivating than any internal drive.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always in good ways. Positive feedback would give me a small boost, but negative feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that type of work altogether. I'd check my scores and either feel relieved or scared about what came next. The gamified elements helped because the feedback was more immediate and felt less personal than getting a bad grade.
Q18: The structure helped in some ways because it gave me a path to follow when I couldn't motivate myself. Having modules laid out meant I could just do the next thing without having to decide what to focus on. But the structure also felt rigid sometimes, and when I fell behind, it was hard to catch up because everything built on what came before. The gamified tasks broke things into smaller pieces which helped.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier because they gave me small wins to work toward. The discussion requirements were good too because they forced me to interact even when I didn't feel like it. What made it harder was when there were too many things due at once or when the materials were too text-heavy for my learning style. I needed more hands-on, interactive content.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is scared and checking obsessively but too paralyzed to actually complete work. I've been there, where I'd log in constantly to check deadlines and look at what was due, but the fear would keep me from actually starting assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just anxiety manifesting as clicking around without accomplishing anything.
Q21: That makes sense to me because a lot of real learning happens offline. Someone might download everything, study from printed notes, practice on paper, and only log in to submit work. Their engagement would look low but they're actually putting in serious effort. I did some of that myself, though my performance was just okay. The LMS only sees part of the picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They miss offline studying, physical note-taking, and all the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't capture fear or stress that drives behavior without showing up as productive work.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's when you're mentally present and working through problems, not just checking boxes. For me, real engagement is hands-on, working through things physically until they make sense. It's hard to feel engaged when you're scared and stressed all the time, even if you're technically doing the work.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how much that fear drove my behavior. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline with handwritten notes and downloaded materials. It wouldn't show the gamified tasks actually helping my mood improve, or how low my motivation was despite logging in regularly. The emotional experience of struggling through this course is completely invisible in the data.
